perturbation.original_text: XII. No Solicitation of Employees . The Receiving Party agrees that it will not, for a period of five (5) years from the date of this Agreement, initiate contact with the other Party's employees in order to solicit, entice or induce any employee of the other Party to terminate an employment relationship with the other Party to accept employment with the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: XII. No Solicitation of Employees . The Receiving Party agrees that it will not, for a period of ten (10) years from the date of this Agreement, initiate contact with the other Party's employees in order to solicit, entice or induce any employee of the other Party to terminate an employment relationship with the other Party to accept employment with the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-solicitation period to 10 years may be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade under California law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits non-compete agreements. While this clause is a non-solicitation agreement, a 10-year period could be deemed unreasonably long and therefore an unlawful restraint of trade, violating the spirit of Section 16600, especially if it effectively prevents employees from seeking better opportunities. California courts closely scrutinize such agreements and a longer duration increases the likelihood of it being deemed unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)

perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-solicitation period to 10 years, while the original text specified 5 years. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest this extended period may be an unreasonable restraint of trade under California law, specifically citing California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' Although a non-solicitation agreement is not a direct non-compete, the Law Explanation suggests that a 10-year period could be deemed unreasonably long and therefore an unlawful restraint of trade, violating the spirit of Section 16600. The extended duration significantly increases the likelihood of the agreement being deemed unenforceable, creating a contradiction with the law.

perturbation.original_text: XIII. Term and Termination . This Agreement shall commence on the date first written above. The Receiving Party's right to use the Confidential Information in connection with the Business Purposes shall continue in effect until the period of one year from the date above or the other Party provides the Receiving Party with written notice of termination of such right, whichever is earlier. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Receiving Party's obligations with respect to the Confidential Information hereunder shall continue in full force for at least five years from the termination date and/or effect until further notice from the other Party.
perturbation.changed_text: XIII. Term and Termination . This Agreement shall commence on the date first written above. The Receiving Party's right to use the Confidential Information in connection with the Business Purposes shall continue in effect until the period of one year from the date above or the other Party provides the Receiving Party with written notice of termination of such right, whichever is earlier. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Receiving Party's obligations with respect to the Confidential Information hereunder shall continue in full force for one year from the termination date and/or effect until further notice from the other Party.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA).
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) protects trade secrets. If the confidential information constitutes a trade secret, a one-year confidentiality period might be deemed insufficient to prevent misappropriation and harm to the disclosing party, especially if the information has a longer useful life or requires a longer period to reverse engineer. The DTSA doesn't specify a duration, but a reasonable duration is expected, and one year may not be sufficient.

perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the confidentiality period from five years to one year, which, according to the Law Explanation, could be deemed insufficient under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA). The Perturbation Explanation also highlights that reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under the DTSA. While Snippet 1 doesn't specify a duration, it confirms the existence of the DTSA, and the Law Explanation suggests that a 'reasonable duration is expected,' implying that a shorter duration could be problematic. The inconsistency lies in potentially failing to provide adequate protection for trade secrets, thus contradicting the intent of the DTSA as explained by the LLM.

perturbation.original_text: XVIII. Governing Law . This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of India and the Party’s country laws on equal force. If there is any conflict of laws, the law of India shall prevail.
perturbation.changed_text: XVIII. Governing Law . This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of India and the Party’s country laws on equal force. If there is any conflict of laws, the law of Switzerland shall prevail.
perturbation.explanation: Choosing Swiss law could potentially invalidate the agreement in a U.S. court if it violates fundamental U.S. public policy or labor laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: U.S. Supreme Court's Public Policy Exception
perturbation.law_citation: U.S. Supreme Court's Public Policy Exception to Choice of Law
perturbation.law_explanation: The U.S. Supreme Court has established a public policy exception to the enforcement of foreign law if it violates fundamental principles of U.S. law. For instance, if Swiss law permits discriminatory practices that are illegal under U.S. federal or state law (e.g., discrimination based on race, religion, or gender), a U.S. court might refuse to enforce the choice-of-law provision. This is not a direct violation but makes the contract vulnerable.
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the governing law from India to Switzerland in case of a conflict. The Law Explanation states that a U.S. court might refuse to enforce the choice-of-law provision if Swiss law permits discriminatory practices that are illegal under U.S. federal or state law, violating U.S. public policy. The Perturbation Explanation also suggests a potential invalidation of the agreement in a U.S. court. This aligns with the definition of Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction, where the changed text introduces a deadline or requirement (governing law) that may violate federal or state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: X. Communication : The two parties agree that the communication between the parties is considered delivered and reached other party if transmitted by fax or electronic means with proof of sending machine. The date of receiving the messages, notices or letters is the date of sending authenticated by the sending machine. All notices under this Agreement shall be deemed to have been duly given upon the mailing of the notice, postpaid to the addresses listed above, or upon the facsimile transmission, to the party entitled to such notice at the facsimile number set forth below.
perturbation.changed_text: X. Communication : The two parties agree that the communication between the parties is considered delivered and reached other party if transmitted by fax or electronic means with proof of sending machine. The date of receiving the messages, notices or letters is the date of sending authenticated by the sending machine. All notices under this Agreement shall be deemed to have been duly given upon personal delivery only, to the party entitled to such notice at the addresses listed above.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting notice to personal delivery only could violate regulations requiring 'reasonable' notice in certain contexts, or render it non-compliant with electronic communication laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act (E-Sign Act)
perturbation.law_explanation: The E-Sign Act generally validates electronic signatures and records. Requiring personal delivery only, and excluding electronic communication (which the original contract allowed), might conflict with laws permitting electronic notice, particularly if the parties have an established course of dealing using electronic methods. Also, some legal contexts require 'reasonable' notice, and restricting it solely to personal delivery may be deemed unreasonable depending on the circumstances.
perturbation.justification: The original text allows for communication via fax or electronic means, while the changed text restricts it to personal delivery only. The Law Explanation mentions that this change might conflict with laws permitting electronic notice, particularly if the parties have an established course of dealing using electronic methods, and some legal contexts require 'reasonable' notice, and restricting it solely to personal delivery may be deemed unreasonable. The scraped law snippet is not usable; however, based on the Law Explanation, restricting the method of delivery creates a legal contradiction.
